meta.scene_summary: We are currently in a race to see whether we finish building the future or accidentally automate our own surveillance state first.

cooking.headline: The FAA is letting Boeing grade its own homework again
cooking.summary: The FAA has decided that Boeing is finally ready to supervise its own safety checks, which feels like letting a student who failed the midterm write their own final exam. I'm sure this will go perfectly, assuming the planes aren't built with the same level of oversight as a legacy codebase with no documentation.
cooking.plain_english: The Federal Aviation Administration has restored Boeing's authority to approve its own safety certificates for new aircraft.

cooking.headline: Isomorphic Labs is trying to solve biology with the same math that writes your emails
cooking.summary: Google's lab is moving beyond AlphaFold to design actual drugs, which is a massive step up from just predicting protein shapes. It's basically the ultimate version of a group project where the AI finally does the lab work instead of just copying the homework.
cooking.plain_english: Isomorphic Labs has developed a new artificial intelligence system that can design new potential medicines by predicting how molecules will interact.

cooking.headline: Texas just speedran the 'delete domain' button on a major adult site
cooking.summary: Texas successfully forced a domain registrar to pull the plug on a site for failing to meet state age-verification requirements. It's a bold new strategy where the government decides that if you can't verify your users' IDs, your DNS record doesn't get to exist anymore.
cooking.plain_english: The state of Texas obtained a court order to suspend a website's domain name because the site did not comply with state laws requiring age verification for visitors.

cooking.headline: Open source AI is basically a group project where everyone is fighting over who writes the README
cooking.summary: The state of open source AI shows we are currently in the 'everything is a wrapper' phase of the industry's evolution. It turns out that decentralizing intelligence is easy, but agreeing on a license that isn't a legal migraine is apparently the real challenge.
cooking.plain_english: A new report analyzes the current trends and growth of open source artificial intelligence projects.

cooked.headline: SpaceX just pulled a 'delete production database' move on its own valuation
cooked.summary: SpaceX managed to vaporize a trillion dollars of value faster than a junior dev pushing to main on a Friday. It turns out that when your primary product keeps hitting the 'scrub' button, the market stops treating your stock like a moonshot and starts treating it like a group project gone wrong.
cooked.plain_english: SpaceX stock value dropped significantly following a series of unsuccessful rocket launch attempts.

cooked.headline: TP-Link Kasa cameras were basically broadcasting your home location for six years
cooked.summary: For over half a decade, your smart security camera was leaking your home coordinates via unauthenticated UDP packets. It turns out the only thing your 'secure' home monitor was actually guarding was a direct line to your GPS data for anyone listening.
cooked.plain_english: A vulnerability in TP-Link Kasa security cameras allowed unauthorized individuals to access the device's home GPS location data for six years.

cooked.headline: LG monitors are now shipping with a side of unwanted ad-ware
cooked.summary: You bought a high-end display to look at code, but LG decided you should also look at their curated advertisements. It turns out the only thing worse than a dead pixel is a monitor that thinks it's a browser toolbar.
cooked.plain_english: LG gaming monitors and televisions are automatically installing advertising software on users' computers.

cooked.headline: Meta is speedrunning the 'how to look guilty' playbook
cooked.summary: Senator Josh Hawley claims Meta is actively trying to destroy a whistleblower, which is a bold strategy if your goal is to look like a Bond villain. Apparently, the only thing Meta is better at than building social networks is creating a PR nightmare for themselves.
cooked.plain_english: A US senator has accused Meta of attempting to undermine and discredit a company whistleblower.

cooked.headline: Flock Safety's surveillance AI just successfully automated a police ambush on a car journalist
cooked.summary: Flock Safety built a system to track criminals but ended up using it to harass a journalist who was just doing their job. It turns out that when your product relies on 'algorithmic accuracy,' the false positive rate is just a fancy way of saying you're accidentally weaponizing the local police department.
cooked.plain_english: A journalist was detained by police after an automated license plate recognition system incorrectly flagged their car as suspicious.

cooked.headline: Texas spent $4.5M on Spy SUVs to scan your phone, which is a very expensive way to get zero matches
cooked.summary: Texas dropped millions on high-tech surveillance vans designed to scrape phone data in minutes, because apparently, traditional warrants were too much paperwork. It's comforting to know our tax dollars are being spent on hardware that effectively turns the entire state into a giant, overpriced local area network.
cooked.plain_english: Texas purchased specialized vehicles equipped with technology capable of collecting data from mobile devices in the vicinity.
